evidence_quotes.quote: the video games will not be destroyed after the responsible party (publisher, developer, etc.) has received payments for the game!

The precedent being set by creators/publishers of video games that they can remove access whenever they'd like, with no warning to the consumer, is one I find dangerous when it comes to consumer protections.
Nintendo and their latest console, the Nintendo Switch 2, where people are experiencing severe restrictions being put upon them for playing secondhand games, as well as modding.

paragraphs.text: Hello! I'd like to ask if it would be possible to add or otherwise look into protections for customers who have purchased video games, namely in the sense that the video games will not be destroyed after the responsible party (publisher, developer, etc.) has received payments for the game! No one expects something to last forever, but in the case of video games, some have been put up for sale only to have their availability be taken away from paying customers within a short timeframe and with no warning (an example of this is for instance the video game "The Crew"). Before the current, highly digital age, you could purchase a video game and keep it for years -- there are consoles from over 20 years ago that still work today, as do their games! The precedent being set by creators/publishers of video games that they can remove access whenever they'd like, with no warning to the consumer, is one I find dangerous when it comes to consumer protections. A further example of this can be seen with regards to Nintendo and their latest console, the Nintendo Switch 2, where people are experiencing severe restrictions being put upon them for playing secondhand games, as well as modding. It's also worth pointing out protections for video games is something a great many citizens care about globally, but particularly within the EU, as demonstrated by the large amount of signatures for the "Stop Killing Games" movement. I hope it is possible to look into consumer protections for the purchase of video games on modern platforms in relation to the Digital Fairness Act. Thank you very much for your time!
